records.display_name: MTA exploring Q West project

payload.claim_text: MTA will take a serious look into the possibility of keeping the tunnel boring machine moving when it reaches the end of its path in this phase of the Second Avenue Subway. MTA will look to see if it may be continued west, perhaps all the way to Broadway to lay the core groundwork for the next phase of Second Avenue Subway. MTA is not committing financially to a project however MTA will explore it and the opportunity to explain what MTA is calling the Q West project, Q to the west across 125th Street.

records.raw_text: MTA will take a serious look into the possibility of keeping the tunnel boring machine moving when it reaches the end of its path in this phase of the Second Avenue Subway...to see if it may be continued west, perhaps all the way to Broadway...MTA is calling the Q West project, Q to the west – across 125th Street.

records.display_name: Sixth Avenue Line next CBTC project

payload.claim_text: The Sixth Avenue Line on the B, D, F, and M trains is the next CBTC project to advance after the Fulton and Liberty Lines CBTC signal modernization.

records.raw_text: He also identified the Sixth Avenue Line on the B, D, F, and M trains as the next CBTC project to advance.

records.display_name: Westchester Bee-Line Bus System on OMNY

payload.claim_text: Board Member Lopez thanked President Torres-Springer and his staff for their help in bringing the West Chester Bee-Line Bus System onto the OMNY system.

records.raw_text: Board Member Lopez thanked President Torres-Springer and his staff for his presentation and for their help in bringing the West Chester Bee-Line Bus System onto the OMNY system.

records.display_name: Governor introduced study for improved Jamaica Station

payload.claim_text: The Governor, in her State of the State address, also introduced the idea of creating a much better Jamaica Station. A study will be conducted jointly with the Port Authority.

records.raw_text: The Governor, in her State of the State address, also introduced the idea of creating a much better Jamaica Station...A study will be conducted jointly with the Port Authority.

records.display_name: New fare gate prototypes tested at 9 stations

payload.claim_text: New fare gate prototypes are getting a lot of attention. Three different models being tested across nine stations. That number of stations is going to double in the weeks to come. More than 2 million people have used them so far. Ultimately new faregates will be installed in 150 stations as part of our 2025-2029 Capital Program. The selected stations will account for over 70% of MTA ridership.

records.raw_text: New fare gate prototypes...three different models, all being paid for by the companies who are competing for this contract. There are three different models being tested across nine stations. That number of stations is going to double in the weeks to come. More than 2 million people have used them so far...Ultimately new faregates will be installed in 150 stations as part of our 2025-2029 Capital Program. The selected stations will account for over 70% of MTA ridership.

records.display_name: New Jamaica Bus Depot

description: Design and construction of a new LEED certified bus depot facility at 165-18 Tuskegee Airmen Way, Queens, accommodating transition to 100% electric bus fleet with 272 Standard Bus Equivalent parking spaces. Contract awarded to Skanska USA Civil Northeast Inc. for $479,956,000.
Design-build services for a new Jamaica Bus Depot, the MTA's first zero-emissions bus facility. Contract awarded to Skanska USA Civil Northeast Inc. for $483,775,300.

Design and construction of new LEED certified bus depot at 165-18 Tuskegee Airmen Way, Queens, with phased demolition of existing depot. Designed for 100% electric bus fleet transition. One-story building with 272 Standard Bus Equivalent parking spaces, three bus washing lanes, two interior bus washing stations, one chassis washing station, fifteen maintenance bays, rooftop bus parking, and new three-story administrative building.

Design and construction of a state-of-the-art bus maintenance and storage facility to replace the existing Jamaica Depot opened in 1939. Facility includes 134,000 SF bus depot, 272 bus parking spots, 15 maintenance bays, 37,000 SF three-story administration building. Capable of charging 60 electric buses, designed for all-electric fleet by 2030.

Design and construction of a new state-of-the-art bus maintenance and storage facility to replace the existing Jamaica Depot, with 272 bus parking spots, 15 maintenance bays, 60 BEB charging capability

135,000-square-foot LEED-certified bus depot with 34,000-square-foot administrative building, accommodating more than 275 buses with charging stations for over 130 electric buses. Currently 10 months behind schedule due to permitting challenges. IEC reports project is 27% complete as of the report period.

New LEED-certified bus depot replacing the 85-year-old structure, including 135,000 sq ft bus facility, 34,000 sq ft administrative building, capacity for 272 buses, 15 maintenance bays, 3 drive-through washers, 3 fueling stations, and 60 BEB charging ports.

Replacement of 85-year-old facility with a modern depot capable of housing 272 buses, supporting full electrification and high-voltage infrastructure. Design-build project. $649 million budget. Forecast substantial completion date of October 2027.

New state-of-the-art bus depot in Jamaica, Queens replacing nearly 90-year-old existing one. Will house nearly 300 new buses with modern bus washing stations, maintenance bays, and green bus infrastructure.
Replacement of an 85-year-old facility with a modern depot capable of housing 272 buses, supporting full electrification and high-voltage infrastructure
Design-build project to replace an 85-year-old facility with a modern depot capable of housing 272 buses, supporting full electrification and high-voltage infrastructure.

payload.description: Design and construction of a new LEED certified bus depot facility at 165-18 Tuskegee Airmen Way, Queens, accommodating transition to 100% electric bus fleet with 272 Standard Bus Equivalent parking spaces. Contract awarded to Skanska USA Civil Northeast Inc. for $479,956,000.

records.raw_text: Work on the Jamaica Bus Depot project, a 135,000-square-foot LEED-certified facility with an additional 34,000-square-foot administrative building, is progressing. The new depot will accommodate more than 275 buses and include charging stations for over 130 electric buses. However, the project is currently 10 months behind schedule due to permitting challenges related to a temporary parking lot at York College.

records.display_name: Queens Interlocking modernization project

description: Modernizing Queens Interlocking, a major junction point connecting the Hempstead Branch with the Main Line, seeing 333 trains operate through it each weekday. Includes improved switch configuration and modernized signal system to provide enhanced routing options, additional capacity and improve OTP through the Main Line Corridor.
Queens Interlocking is a major junction point that operates 333 trains daily and joins the Hempstead Branch to the Main Line. Upcoming work will require service modifications for three weekends.
Modernization of a major junction point operating 333 trains daily joining the Hempstead Branch to the Main Line; upcoming work requires service modifications for three weekends

payload.description: Modernizing Queens Interlocking, a major junction point connecting the Hempstead Branch with the Main Line, seeing 333 trains operate through it each weekday. Includes improved switch configuration and modernized signal system to provide enhanced routing options, additional capacity and improve OTP through the Main Line Corridor.

records.raw_text: We constantly work to modernize and expand our system, which has led to us to update Queens Interlocking, a major junction point in our system connecting the Hempstead Branch with our Main Line and seeing 333 trains operate through it each weekday. Once complete, the improved switch configuration and modernized signal system will provide enhanced routing options, additional capacity and improve OTP through this vital segment of the Main Line Corridor.

records.display_name: Interborough Express

description: Transformative Light Rail Transit Project connecting communities in Brooklyn and Queens to 17 subway lines and the Long Island Railroad
Formal engineering of the Interborough Express
Capital project under MTA Capital Construction & Development

Part of the MTA Expansion Program with $58.3 million added to the 2020-2024 Capital Program. Element # G80801, new project with $58,261,183 budget. Funded by $43 million from the State of New York (increasing envelope) and $15 million from the federal RAISE grant program.

Full design beginning, released RFP for preliminary engineering team to advance the project. With 2025-2029 capital plan funding, will be able to complete design for NYC's first light rail line serving more than 100,000 daily riders, connecting neighborhoods in Brooklyn and Queens.
Full design beginning; RFP released for preliminary engineering team. NYC's first light rail line, will serve more than 100,000 daily riders and connect neighborhoods in Brooklyn and Queens.
NYC's first light rail line which will serve more than 100,000 daily riders and connect neighborhoods in Brooklyn and Queens
NYC's first light rail line serving more than 100,000 daily riders connecting neighborhoods in Brooklyn and Queens

Transformative light rail train project in existing rail corridor between Bay Ridge and Jackson Heights. C&D is pursuing FTA funding, engaging public outreach including 35+ meetings. Design progressing with survey and design work prioritizing minimizing bridge reconstruction, improving subway connections, reducing station costs. Next steps include continuing outreach and preparing RFP for preliminary engineering.
The proposed 2025-2029 Capital Plan includes funding for the Interborough Express (IBX).
MTA C&D project listed in Federal FFY 2025 grant request
NYS Budget to Fund Capital Plan - Interborough Express route in Brooklyn from Roosevelt Avenue to Brooklyn Army Terminal

Transformative new rapid transit service between Brooklyn and Queens. Transforming a lightly used freight railway into a light-rail line serving almost 1 million nearby residents and connecting 17 subway lines, more than 50 bus routes and Long Island Rail Road.
MTA Interborough Express - Advancing the IBX
Light rail service connecting Brooklyn and Queens, delivered in two phases. Phase 1 prepares the corridor; Phase 2 installs the light rail system.
Light rail transit connecting Brooklyn and Queens from Sunset Park to Jackson Heights, 19 stations, 14-mile corridor, approximately 32 minute end-to-end runtime, projected daily ridership of 160,000.
Environmental review underway, running concurrently with the design contract awarded in August. First public meeting of the environmental review will take place at Brooklyn College.
$58.3 million to support design for the Interborough Express project (IBX)
Formal kickoff of design and engineering for the Interborough Express project
Formal kickoff of design and engineering for the Interborough Express project, with anticipated collaboration with the City.
Large scale expansion project referenced during public hearing for FFY 2026. Public hearing comments noted significant support for the Interborough Express (IBX).
Transit link serving historically underserved communities in Brooklyn and Queens, connecting them to subway, bus, and LIRR, serving 900,000 residents
Light rail transit system along an existing rail corridor from Sunset Park to Jackson Heights, approximately 14 miles with 18 stations, ~32 minute end-to-end runtime, 160,000 projected daily riders.
Next Phase of IBX Outreach

New transit connection linking Brooklyn and Queens along existing 14-mile freight rail corridor between Sunset Park and Jackson Heights. 18 stations, connections to 17 subway lines, 50+ bus routes, and LIRR. Light rail selected as preferred mode in 2023. Design RFP issued 2024. Included in MTA Capital Plan 2025, not yet fully funded. Environmental review/DEIS anticipated later this year (2026).
Next phase of IBX outreach advancing quickly with another six community workshops. Full details of events on MTA website.
Proposed 14-mile light rail line connecting Brooklyn waterfront at Bay Ridge with Jackson Heights Queens
status: full design beginning, RFP released for preliminary engineering team
full design to begin; RFP released for preliminary engineering team
proposed for federal funding in FFY 2025
preliminary design contract award pending Board approval
design development and environmental review underway

records.raw_text: an addition of $58.3 million for the Interborough Express project

records.display_name: Rockaway Line Resiliency and Rehabilitation

description: Rehabilitation and flood resiliency work along NYCT's Rockaway Line including viaduct repairs at Hammels Wye Campus, flood mitigation, new signal tower at Beach 105th Street Station, and replacement of electrical/mechanical components of the South Channel Bridge. Contract awarded to Schiavone Construction Co. LLC for $392,685,186.

Rehabilitation and flood resiliency work along NYCT Rockaway Line in Queens. Includes viaduct repairs at Hammels Wye Campus and along east/west branches, flood mitigation elements, new signal tower, track crossover at Beach 105th Street Station, replacement of South Channel Bridge electrical/mechanical components.

Long-term flood mitigation project for Rockaway Line including room hardening at Howard Beach, Broad Channel and Beach 116th St, debris shields, signal tower and crossover at Beach 105th St Station, replacement of electrical/mechanical equipment on South Channel Bridge, Hammels Wye Viaduct Rehabilitation, and Rockaway Line Viaduct Rehabilitation.

Long-term flood mitigation project protecting critical assets along the Rockaway Line providing A train service. Includes room hardening, debris shields, signal tower and crossover at Beach 105th St Station, replacement of electrical/mechanical equipment on South Channel Bridge, Hammels Wye Viaduct Rehabilitation, and Rockaway Line Viaduct Rehabilitation.
Resiliency improvements and rehabilitation of the Rockaway Line viaduct

Long-term flood mitigation project to protect critical assets along the Rockaway Line, bundled with viaduct rehabilitation and upgrades to South Channel Bridge, including Hammels Wye demolition and replacement

Rockaway Line Resiliency project. On schedule and within budget. Four-month-long general service diversion implemented. Track, signal, and communication work at Beach 105th Street temporarily halted for technical evaluation.

Long-term flood mitigations along the Rockaway Line carrying the A train through Jamaica Bay estuary and to the Rockaway Peninsula, bundled with viaduct rehabilitation and upgrades to the South Channel Bridge.

Rockaway Line resiliency and viaduct rehabilitation project; within budget with estimated completion cost of $584 million. Signal system improvements at Beach 105th Street Station have a forecast delay of approximately 10 months.

Reconstruction of Hammels Wye, protective shielding, track rebuilding, and resiliency improvements to protect A Line service against extreme weather in the Rockaways; estimated completion cost $584 million
project_name: Rockaway Line Resiliency and Viaduct Rehabilitation

payload.description: Rehabilitation and flood resiliency work along NYCT's Rockaway Line including viaduct repairs at Hammels Wye Campus, flood mitigation, new signal tower at Beach 105th Street Station, and replacement of electrical/mechanical components of the South Channel Bridge. Contract awarded to Schiavone Construction Co. LLC for $392,685,186.

records.raw_text: Mr. Peyyetti noted that the Rockaway Line Resiliency project remains on schedule and within budget.

records.display_name: Line structure component repairs - Seventh and Eighth Avenue Lines

payload.description: Design-bid-build project addressing critical defects on the Seventh and Eighth Avenue Lines through targeted steel and concrete repairs to columns, beams, and tunnel walls. Affects five Manhattan stations. Includes stormwater mitigation, demolition of abandoned structures, and repairs to girders and abutments. Substantial completion forecast for January 2028.
payload.project_name: Line structure component repairs - Seventh and Eighth Avenue Lines

records.display_name: Second Avenue Subway Phase 2

description: Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway. Committed $221 million as of December 2021. Initial funding $1.735 billion. FTA approved entry into Engineering phase on January 6, 2022.

Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway extending Q service north to 125th Street and Lexington Avenue. Includes Contracts 2, 3 and 4: tunnels and stations between 115th St and 125th St, 106th St station structure, and ancillary buildings/fit-out.

Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway extending Q service north in Manhattan to 125th Street and Lexington Avenue. Contracts 2, 3 and 4 include construction of tunnels, stations at 106th, 116th and 125th Streets, and ancillary facilities.
Advancing Second Ave Subway Phase 2 with stations at 106th Street, 116th Street, and 125th Street
Added funding to support the federally-required contingency
Approved capital plan amendment to put MTA in position to issue RFP for utility relocation contract, the start of Phase 2.
Continued progress on the Second Avenue Subway noted as a significant project. 2015-2019 Capital Program priority projects included Second Avenue Subway Phase 2.
Phase 2 will extend the Q line into Harlem by creating new stations on Second Avenue at 106th, 116th, and 125th Streets. Field office at 159 East 125th Street expected operational by April 2024.
Eminent domain procedures and property acquisitions in connection with Second Avenue Subway Phase 2

Phase 2 will extend the Q line into Harlem creating new stations on Second Avenue at 106th, 116th, and 125th Streets. Field office lease at 159 East 125th Street approved for SAS2 design and buildout period.
Even as we start the Early Works, future contracts need congestion pricing.
Strengthen & Expand the Network
Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway Project will extend the Q line into Harlem and improve commutes for area residents by creating new stations on Second Avenue at 106th, 116th, and 125th Streets.
Field office lease at 159 East 125th Street, New York, NY for the Second Avenue Subway Phase II project. 17,647 SF on third floor. Letter agreement for design work reimbursement up to $225,000.

Acquisition of temporary and permanent easements for subsurface space beneath NYPL Property, PS 96 Property, and RFK Approach Property for construction and operation of subway tunnels and ancillary facilities
Second Avenue Subway Phase 2 will move forward with congestion pricing funding.

Moving forward with restoration of congestion pricing; on track to meet planned revenue service date; moving immediately into major stage of contracting for tunnel construction; identified $1.3 billion in savings compared to initial project plan estimate.
Strengthen & Expand the Network - Keeping Second Ave Subway Phase 2 on Track

Will extend from northern limit of SAS Phase 1 at 105 St, north under Second Ave, then west along 125 St. Three new stations at 106 St, 116 St, and 125 St. Connection to Lexington Avenue Line (4,5,6) and Metro-North Railroad at 125 St.

Will service 100,000 riders per day and reduce commute times by up to 20 minutes. Substantial completion and budget remain on track. Lessons learned from Phase 1 saved $1.3 billion. Team will secure access to utility agreements and easements.
Program management contract with financial incentives and penalties; design contract converted to design-bid-build to minimize risk and save tens of millions of dollars
Acquisition of property for Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway Project
Property acquisition for Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway, at 124-130 W 125th St for Ancillary A vertical shaft construction
Second Avenue Subway Phase 2 Contract 2 Award presentation covering tunnels, stations, and contract schedule
Extend Q line from 105 St north under Second Ave then west along 125 St, with three new stations at 106 St, 116 St, and 125 St. Budget $6.968B. On time and on budget.
Second Avenue Subway Phase 2 with $5.233 billion in the 2020-2024 Capital Program, unchanged in Amendment #5
Subway project funded through CRZ program as part of 2020-2024 Capital Program
MTA continuing to advance Second Avenue Subway Phase 2; tunneling contract awarded with approximately $700 million in labor savings and $1.3 billion in total identified savings

Acquisition of subsurface permanent easement and construction access license at 2340 Second Avenue (Senator Robert F. Wagner Senior Houses) for the construction of subway tunnels under Contract 2 of Phase 2
Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway project extending north from 125th Street including tunneling from 120th Street to 125th Street, with new stations at 106th Street, 116th Street, and 125th Street

Second Avenue Subway Phase 2 project advancement including tunneling contract award with approximately $700 million in labor savings and $1.3 billion in total identified savings. Exploration of extending tunnel boring machine west across 125th Street (Q West project).

Property acquisition for subsurface easement (19,314 sq. ft.) for tunnel and construction access license (28,442 sq. ft.) for ground improvement work at NYCHA's Senator Robert F. Wagner Senior Houses complex at 2340 Second Avenue. Easement $640,000, license $180,000.
MTA is fighting for Second Avenue Subway Phase 2, with checkmark indicating progress
Contract C26203 for design-build services to excavate and construct structural shells for 106th Street Station and connecting tunnels for Phase 2 of Second Avenue Subway
Extends the Q from East 96 to 125 Streets, will serve 100,000 daily riders
Will extend Q train service from 96 St to 125 St and Lexington Avenue, with three new ADA-accessible stations at 106 St, 116 St, and 125 St, serving over 100,000 daily riders.
SAS2 Funding Restored

MTA filed a lawsuit to compel federal government to release funding for Phase 2. Preparatory work adjacent to the Metro-North station at 125th Street and Park Avenue is underway to create space for the tunnel boring machine.
Property acquisition for subway tunnel construction beneath NYCHA's Senator Robert F. Wagner Senior Houses complex

Extension of the Second Avenue Subway north to East Harlem. Four major contracts with combined budget of $6.968 billion. Four contracts: Contract 1 (utility relocation), Contract 2 (design-build tunnel), Contract 3 (open trench, five blocks 60-ft deep, 105th-110th Streets), Contract 4 (station interiors/systems). Forecasted revenue service date September 2032.

Chair Lieber reported restoration of funding for Second Avenue Subway Phase 2 by the federal government. A hiring fair had high turnout with East Harlem residents showing strong interest in MTA jobs and construction apprenticeship opportunities.
Design-Build contract for Phase 2 of Second Avenue Subway, including adapting existing tunnel at 116th Street, constructing new tunnel to 125th Street, and building two stations
Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway Project running approximately from 96th Street and 2nd Avenue northwards to 125th Street, then westwards along 125th Street to just past Lenox Avenue

Extension of Q service from 96th Street to 125th Street and Lexington Avenue. Contract 2 covers construction of launch box for TBM, bored tunnels from 120th Street to 125th Street, and mined 125th Street Station.

Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway Project extending SAS from 96th Street to 125th Street in Manhattan. Contract for real estate relocation services awarded to O.R. Colan Associates, LLC for $11,052,964 (72 months).
Contract C81439 to O.R. Colan Associates, LLC for 72 months at $11,052,964 (not-to-exceed). Extends from 96th Street to 125th Street in Manhattan.

Procurement of up to 1,729 buses (952 for NYC Transit and 777 for MTA Bus Company) through multiple competitive RFPs, including 1,042 low-floor 40-foot buses, 345 low-floor 60-foot buses, and 342 over-the-road express buses; 485 of the low-floor buses will be all-electric
Property interests acquisition for Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway Project, including temporary easements for excavation support from 105th Street to 125th Street and west along 125th Street

RFP authorizing resolution for purchase of up to 1,729 buses (952 NYCT + 777 MTA Bus Company), remaining in 2020-2024 Capital Plan. Includes 1,042 low-floor 40-ft, 345 low-floor 60-ft, 342 over-the-road express buses; 485 all-electric (460 NYCT, 25 MTABC). Replaces buses at end of 12-year useful life.

Acquisition of property from the City for Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway. Property located on east side of Park Avenue between 124th and 125th Streets, to be used as entrance for Second Avenue Subway and an ancillary facility including a substation.
Acquisition of temporary easement property interests for support of excavation for the new sub-surface tunnel from 105th Street to 125th Street, then west along 125th Street to just past Lenox Avenue
$4.555 billion in the 2020-2024 Capital Program for Phase 2 of Second Avenue Subway, unchanged from the approved program.

Board authorization to acquire permanent and temporary easements for construction of Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway. Easements primarily needed for support of excavation, ground support, and improvement work.

Acquisition of permanent and temporary easements for construction of Phase 2 of NYCT's Second Avenue Subway Project, for new subway tunnel from 105th Street to 125th Street, then west along 125th Street to just past Lenox Avenue

Proposed Phase 2 of the Second Avenue Subway, extending from 96th Street to 125th Street, with proposed stations at 106th Street, 116th Street, 125th Street-2nd Avenue (serving the proposed 2nd Avenue/Queens Boulevard U line), Lexington Avenue-125th Street, Malcolm X Boulevard (Lenox Avenue)-125th Street, Saint Nicholas Avenue-125th Street, and Broadway-125th Street.
